Hi, I'm Jordan Gunn, a postdoc here since September. I build computational models of human memory — models that simulate how people search through and retrieve their past experiences. Here I want to give a sense of how memory search models help us clarify and refine our understanding of mental life.

Imagine you’ve recently started frequenting your neighborhood bakery. On Monday you walk in, smell cinnamon, and buy a fresh loaf. A week later you go back—now it’s espresso in the air and a new cashier. Weeks after that, a curious friend asks if you’ve ever visited that bakery down the street.

Hopefully, your friend’s question is enough a good cue that basic facts come to mind: you can recall that you’ve visited that bakery at all, and even that you’ve visited it two times rather than once. At the same time, hopefully the details of each visit don’t bleed together, and if your friend is interested, you can describe either visit separately without confusing which things happened when. 

Scenarios like these suggest that memory systems must carefully navigate two competing demands.
On the one hand, they must bridge across related memories so that partial cues in the present can call back relevant details from the past.
But on the other hand, they must also be able to differentiate between stored experiences so that one moment’s details are not confused with those of another.

The list learning paradigm helps us study this in the lab. We have participants study a sequence, and the same item -- say the letter D -- appears twice: once early, once later. Here, instead of sights and smells, the contextual details of occurrence are the neighborhood they occur in —the different items before and after. 

By asking people to recall details of these sequence, we can get a sense of how people bridge and differentiate their memories across these occurrences.

In the serial recall task, we can ask people to remember lists in the exact order they were presented. So letter D has to be remembered twice, and each time be followed by the correct neighbor from its study position. If, for example, people mix up when they report one neighbor or the other, we can interpret that as a sign that they have trouble keeping each study event’s contextual details separate!

Retrieved-context models are able to make really detailed predictions about how memory works in scenarios like these.

The proposal behind them is fundamentally the simple idea that item features and their temporal contexts are represented in our minds and associated with each other in memory such that one can call back the other to support sequential recall. 

Each time you study an item, you retrieve its contextual associations and integrate it into the ongoing temporal context. This makes context a continuously evolving representation weighted to reflect your most recent experiences. 

Later, you can use your context representation at retrieval to search for and try to recall associated items from the list. Each time you recall an item, you reinstate its associated context, updating your cue before you try recalling another item.

Retrieved-context theory sounds simple, but formalized in a complete model, it can make pretty exact predictions about how
